questions.question: What defines 'problem anger' according to this lesson?

options.text: Feeling angry at all, since anger is always harmful
Only anger that leads to physical violence
Anger defined by its intensity, frequency, duration, and negative impact on your life
Anger directed at strangers rather than loved ones

questions.explanation: Problem anger is not defined by feeling angry — anger is normal and useful. It's defined by four factors: intensity (going from 0 to 100 instantly), frequency (angry most days), duration (staying angry for hours or days), and impact (hurting relationships, work, health, or causing regret).

questions.question: What does research say about venting anger (yelling, hitting a pillow, punching a bag)?

options.text: Venting is the most effective way to release anger
Venting works, but only if done in private
Venting does not reduce anger and can actually make it worse
Venting only works for mild anger, not intense anger

questions.explanation: A large meta-analysis of 154 studies found that arousal-boosting activities like venting are ineffective or even counterproductive for reducing anger. What actually works is calming your nervous system down, not revving it up. Techniques like deep breathing, relaxation, and mindfulness are evidence-based alternatives.

questions.question: Why is the 3-5 range on the anger intensity scale considered the 'sweet spot' for intervention?

options.text: Because anger below 3 is not real anger
Because your thinking brain is still online and you have more options for responding
Because anger above 5 is impossible to control
Because other people only notice your anger at level 3

questions.explanation: Most effective anger management happens in the 3-5 range because your prefrontal cortex (thinking brain) is still functioning and you have more options for how to respond. By the time anger reaches 7+, your thinking brain is largely offline and your body has taken over, making behavior more automatic and harder to control.

questions.question: What does it mean when anger is described as a 'secondary emotion'?

options.text: Anger is less important than other emotions
Anger only occurs after a primary emotion like hurt, and it arrives because it feels more powerful and less vulnerable
Anger is the second emotion you feel every day
Anger is always caused by another person's secondary behavior

questions.explanation: Sometimes anger is not the first thing you feel — it's a reaction to something deeper. For example, finding out a friend talked behind your back might first trigger hurt (the pain of betrayal), but anger arrives within seconds because it feels more powerful and less vulnerable than hurt. Understanding this helps you address what's really driving your reactions.

questions.question: Which of the following is a healthy function of anger?

options.text: Intimidating others so they comply with your wishes
Proving that you are always right in a disagreement
Signaling that a boundary has been crossed and motivating you to stand up for yourself
Allowing you to say whatever you want without consequences

questions.explanation: Healthy anger serves important functions including boundary protection (alerting you when someone has crossed a line), injustice detection (pointing toward situations that need to be addressed), self-advocacy (giving you energy to assert yourself), and motivation (fuel for positive change). When anger is proportionate and expressed constructively, it's useful information.

title: Understanding Anger: Normal vs. Problem Anger - Quiz
